title: Deodorant on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes fresh white mark; chronic yellow takes more rounds
stainChemistry: TWO different deodorant stains on polyester, with different protocols. (1) FRESH WHITE TRANSFER — dry deodorant stick rubbed off onto the shirt as you pulled it on. This is a SURFACE MECHANICAL transfer — aluminum-chlorohydrate powder sitting on polyester fibers. Comes off easily with a soft brush + dilute vinegar rinse before laundering. (2) CHRONIC YELLOW — cumulative buildup over many wears: aluminum salts + sweat proteins + oils bonded to polyester through dozens of wash cycles, yellowed by heat oxidation. Much harder; requires vinegar soak + Dawn + enzyme pretreat + multiple cold-wash rounds. The #1 home mistake: treating chronic yellow as a fresh white transfer — surface wipe won't budge cumulative buildup. Identify which variant you have first.
whyThisWorks: FRESH WHITE: soft brush + dilute vinegar dissolves the aluminum salt surface transfer; cold wash rinses it out. CHRONIC YELLOW: vinegar is the specific acid that chelates aluminum-chlorohydrate bonds to polyester. Dawn emulsifies the oil carrier + surface residue. Enzyme pretreat digests the sweat-protein fraction of the stain. Polyester is OLEOPHILIC (grabs oil), so the oil component is what makes chronic yellow so persistent — surfactant + acid + enzyme in combination addresses all three faces. Fabric softener is the silent saboteur — locks residue in forever. Prevention (aluminum-free deodorant, drying deodorant fully before dressing) matters as much as treatment.
defaultAssumption: FRESH = gym shirt, workout top, jersey — just pulled over head and white streaks from the stick are visible. CHRONIC = polyester workout shirt / uniform with yellow underarm halos that have built up over months. Identify which you have before picking protocol.

spottingProtocol.agent: Identify Variant — White Fresh vs Yellow Chronic
spottingProtocol.instruction: FRESH WHITE transfer (recent, visible white streaks from stick) = short protocol, below. CHRONIC YELLOW (cumulative buildup, yellowed underarm after many wears) = longer protocol, skip to step 5. Don't confuse them — white treatment on yellow wastes time.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Soft brush (old toothbrush) or damp sponge edge
spottingProtocol.instruction: Brush off the white transfer with a soft brush or damp sponge edge. Outward from center. Gets most of it off before chemistry.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 part white vinegar + 2 parts cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute vinegar on damp cloth, press on the spot 5 min. Dissolves any remaining aluminum salt transfer. Blot.

Cold wash per care label. NO fabric softener — softener locks aluminum residue in. Half cup of white vinegar in the rinse cycle instead = natural softener + neutralizes detergent alkalinity. Air-dry or tumble-low; inspect BEFORE high heat.

Chronic yellow underarm on polyester is aluminum salt + sweat protein + oil built up over many wears. One treatment may not fully clear cumulative buildup — budget 2–3 full rounds. ALSO: going forward, switch to aluminum-free deodorant on the shirt you want to preserve, and STOP using fabric softener.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Straight white vinegar (or 1:2 dilute for very old polyester)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pour straight white vinegar on the yellow area (or soak the underarm in 1 cup vinegar + 2 cups cool water for whole-garment). Dwell 15–20 min for fresh buildup, up to 1 hour for heavy. Vinegar's acid breaks aluminum-chlorohydrate bonds — the specific chemistry, no substitute.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Never mix vinegar with bleach or peroxide in the same dwell. Rinse fully between.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Dawn in cool water, work in with soft toothbrush. Lifts dissolved aluminum + oil carrier (polyester is oleophilic — oil is what makes chronic yellow so persistent).

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Zout per label, warm water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Enzyme pretreat per label, warm (not hot) water, 20–30 min dwell. Digests sweat-protein buildup. Skip this step on fresh white — only needed for chronic yellow.

Cold wash per care label. NO fabric softener — softener is the #1 reason deodorant buildup comes back wash after wash. Half cup vinegar in rinse cycle instead. Air-dry — heat sets residual aluminum salts into polyester permanently. Repeat steps 6–9 if yellow remains after air-dry inspection.

steps: 1. Identify — white fresh transfer or chronic yellow buildup.
2. Fresh: soft brush + dilute vinegar spot + cold wash no softener.
3. Chronic: vinegar dwell 15–60 min + Dawn + enzyme pretreat + cold wash.
4. NO fabric softener — use white vinegar in rinse cycle instead.
5. Inspect BEFORE dryer. Heat sets residual.
6. 2–3 full rounds for stubborn cumulative buildup.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool to warm (<100°F); never hot on yellowed polyester
warnings: Fresh white vs chronic yellow = different protocols
NEVER fabric softener — locks deodorant into polyester
NEVER hot water or dryer heat on chronic yellow — fuses aluminum salt permanently
NEVER mix vinegar with bleach or peroxide in same dwell
Polyester is oleophilic — oil fraction matters
Prevention: aluminum-free deodorant + dry before dressing

homeSolutions.agent: Which One Do You Have — Fresh White or Chronic Yellow?
homeSolutions.instruction: There are TWO different deodorant stains on polyester, and they need different treatment. FRESH WHITE streaks (you just pulled the shirt on and left marks from the stick) = a quick surface fix. CHRONIC YELLOW underarm halos (your gym shirt has been yellowing over months) = cumulative buildup that takes multiple rounds. Identify which you have first — treating chronic yellow like a fresh streak just wastes your time.

homeSolutions.agent: Fresh White Streaks — Brush + Vinegar + Cold Wash
homeSolutions.instruction: For fresh white transfer: lift the visible white off with a soft brush (old toothbrush) or the damp edge of a sponge. Brush outward from center. Then mix 1 part white vinegar + 2 parts cool water, dab on a damp cloth, press on the spot 5 minutes. Cold wash the shirt with NO fabric softener. Add half a cup of vinegar to the rinse cycle instead. That's the whole fix — usually one round. Skip the chronic-yellow steps below.

Chronic yellow on polyester is aluminum salt + sweat protein + oil built up over MANY wears. One treatment rarely clears months of buildup — plan for 2 to 3 full rounds. Two things to change going forward or the yellow comes right back: (1) switch to aluminum-free deodorant on the shirts you care about, (2) STOP using fabric softener — it's the #1 reason deodorant buildup is locked in.

Pour WHITE VINEGAR straight on the yellow area (or soak just the underarm in 1 cup vinegar + 2 cups cool water for whole-garment). Let it dwell 15 to 20 minutes for fresh yellowing, up to an HOUR for heavy cumulative buildup. Vinegar's mild acid is THE specific tool that breaks the aluminum-chlorohydrate bond holding the stain to polyester. No household substitute does this job — vinegar is the whole fight here.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix vinegar with bleach or peroxide in the same dwell — chlorine gas. Rinse fully between if you plan to use peroxide next.

homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn into the vinegar-dwelled area with a soft toothbrush. Short strokes, not back-and-forth rubbing. 5 minutes. Polyester is oleophilic — it grabs oil and holds it. Dawn emulsifies the oil carrier so the dissolved aluminum rinses away cleanly instead of re-depositing.

homeSolutions.agent: Chronic Yellow — Enzyme Pretreat for Set Protein
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout per label and dwell 20–30 minutes. Handles the sweat-protein fraction that vinegar and Dawn don't. Skip this on fresh stains — only needed for set yellow. Warm (not hot) water — hot fuses aluminum salt into polyester.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Wash, NO Fabric Softener Ever
homeSolutions.instruction: Cold machine wash with your normal detergent. Do NOT use fabric softener — it's the #1 reason deodorant buildup comes back. Add HALF A CUP OF WHITE VINEGAR to the rinse cycle — it works as a natural softener AND neutralizes detergent alkalinity that could set residual aluminum.

Hang or lay flat to air-dry. Inspect underarms in daylight. Yellow remaining? Repeat steps 4–7. Cumulative buildup often needs 2 or 3 rounds. DO NOT tumble-dry on heat until fully clear — dryer heat fuses residual aluminum salts into polyester permanently.

If cumulative yellow survives 2–3 home rounds, or has been through dryer heat over months (aluminum salts fused into polyester), take it in. Tell them: "Cumulative aluminum + sweat buildup on polyester, treated with vinegar soak + Dawn + enzyme + cold wash. No fabric softener used (going forward). Residual yellow remains." Pro cleaners can apply stronger acetic acid + commercial enzyme sequences. For very set buildup, the shirt may be past recovery — consider replacing and switching to aluminum-free deodorant for the replacement.

steps: 1. Identify — fresh white OR chronic yellow.
2. FRESH: brush + dilute vinegar + cold wash no softener. Done.
6. Cold wash + half-cup vinegar in rinse cycle.
7. Air-dry — inspect BEFORE any dryer heat.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if chronic yellow survives 3 rounds (shirt may be past recovery) or if dryer heat over months has fused aluminum salts permanently.
warnings: Fresh vs chronic are different protocols
NEVER fabric softener — locks deodorant into polyester
NEVER hot water / dryer heat on yellow — fuses aluminum
NEVER mix vinegar with bleach or peroxide (chlorine gas)
Prevention — aluminum-free deodorant + dry before dressing

neverDo: Never fabric softener on polyester with deodorant buildup
Never hot water or dryer heat on yellowed polyester
Never rub aggressively — grinds aluminum into fiber
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs and holds oil tenaciously
Heat-sensitive for this specific stain — dryer sets residual permanently
Color-stable polyester tolerates peroxide if needed (hidden test)

materialWarnings: Two different stains in one card — identify first
Fabric softener is the silent saboteur
Vinegar is the specific chemistry — no substitute

escalation.when: Chronic yellow survives 3 home rounds, shirt has been through dryer heat over months (aluminum fused), or buildup is on a uniform / technical fabric that can't tolerate vinegar-dwell.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cumulative antiperspirant buildup on polyester, treated with vinegar soak + Dawn + enzyme + cold wash. No fabric softener used going forward. [Residual yellow / dryer-heat-fused]. Any hope before I replace?"

contextQuestions: Fresh white streak (today) or chronic yellow (months)?
Polyester workout shirt / uniform / dress shirt?
Has fabric softener been used on this garment?
Has the shirt been through a hot dryer with visible yellow already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The fresh vs chronic fork is the whole card. Fabric softener + antiperspirant is the pair that creates chronic yellow — switching deodorant + ditching softener prevents recurrence. Aluminum-free deodorants don't cause this stain at all.
